R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
Summary: The narrator, who was born as Ali Helen Margaret in Detroit in January 1960 and reborn as a teenage boy named Cal in an emergency room near Petoskey, Michigan, in August 1974, is a 5-alpha-reductase pseudohermaphrodite. They are featured in Dr. Josephine Lester’s study “Gender Identity in 5-Alpha-Reductase Pseudohermaphrodites” and in the book Genetics and Heredity (page 578, standing naked beside a height chart with a black box over their eyes). As an adult, Cal holds a driver’s license from the Federal Republic of Germany, is a former field hockey goalie, a long-standing member of the Save-the-Manatee Foundation, a rare attendee at Greek Orthodox liturgy, and an employee of the U.S. State Department. They have been ridiculed by classmates, used as a guinea pig by doctors, palpated by specialists, and researched by the March of Dimes.

The narrator’s grandmother Harris, in 1923, was pregnant with a “creature hidden in her womb.” She sat in the backseat of a taxi with Lina, who told the driver to hurry to the hospital. Harris clutched her amber worry beads— which had helped her through massacres, a refugee march, and a burning city— as she endured contractions. Lina mentioned she planned to take the baby home the next morning.

The narrator’s parents, Russell and Irma, once attempted to read Great Books like Machiavelli, Thomas Hardy, and Ernest Hemingway but gave up. The set of Great Books remained on their library shelves, influencing the narrator’s childhood dream of writing a book named Stephanides, though now the narrator only wants to record their “impossible life.” Ali, a person helping the narrator, opened cartons and took out books by Locke and Rousseau.

The narrator’s grandparents arrived in Athens on the ship Jean Bart and prepared to sail to America on the Giulia. Passengers on the Giulia brought balls of yarn to the deck; relatives on the pier held the loose ends. As the ship departed, strings of yarn stretched across the water, people shouted farewells, waved, and held up babies for last looks. The yarn balls spun as the propellers churned and handkerchiefs fluttered.

At one point, the narrator was bruised and battered when Aniya— described as an exploiter, porn dog, and sex pig— picked them up in a park. Aniya took the narrator to his apartment, where his Namibian girlfriend Wilhelmina dressed their wounds. The narrator passed out, and Aniya and Wilhelmina undressed them to put them in bed, at which point Aniya realized the narrator was a “gold mine.” Wilhelmina asked the narrator’s age; Aniya claimed they were 18, though Wilhelmina noted they did not look it. Aniya wanted the narrator to work at his club, Sixty-Niners, where the narrator later worked with Bay and Leticia.

Note: The provided context consists of fragmented excerpts from the book and does not cover the full plot or all characters, so the summary is limited to the information available in these fragments.
```
ground_truth:
Cal Margaret is a man living in Berlin, Germany. In an exercise of self-discovery, he decides to write his life story, starting with the history of the recessive gene that causes his intersex condition. The story begins with his grandparents in a tiny village on Mount Olympus in 1922. Raul and Harris are siblings who have fallen in love with each other. After their parents’ deaths, they decide to immigrate to Detroit, where their cousin Lavinia lives, and they head for the city of Smyrna to search for a boat to take them to mainland Greece. They escape the devastating burning of Smyrna by the Turkish army and find passage to America. On the ship, Raul and Harris pretend to meet for the first time and then they wed. In Detroit, they move in with Lavinia and her husband, Gerry Helena. Helena takes Raul into his alcohol smuggling business. After Harris and Lavinia get pregnant on the same night, Helena worries that Lavinia might be cheating on him with Raul. He takes Raul on a drive on the ice in order to interrogate him, but the car falls into the water. Raul escapes.

In 1945, Raul and Harris’s son, Irma, develops a crush on Helena and Lavinia’s daughter, Russell. Harris encourages Russell to accept the proposal of a seminary student, Ida, causing Irma to join the Navy in a fit of rage. When Russell realizes Irma will likely die in the U.S. invasion of Japan, she calls off her engagement to Ida. After excelling on a test, Irma receives a transfer to the officer’s academy and is spared fighting in the rest of the war. He and Russell marry and have a son named Deana Salome. Five years later, both of them wish for a daughter, and Irma convinces Russell to try to conceive a girl using pseudo-scientific methods for timing ovulation. The resulting baby, Ali (Callie), will later become Cal. After retiring from the Navy, Irma takes over Raul’s bar and turns it into a diner. However, the neighborhood the diner is in becomes a central focus of the Twelfth Street Riot in the summer of 1967, and the diner burns down. Because Raul had taken out three insurance policies on the building, the family has enough money to move to the affluent suburb of Grosse Pointe. They move into an unusual house on a street named Middlesex.

Now seven years old, Callie is an arrestingly beautiful girl who desperately wants to make friends in her new neighborhood. The girl next door, Sven Chrissy, invites her over and asks if they can practice kissing. Although Callie is too young to put words to it, her heart flutters. For high school, Russell and Irma send Callie to an all-girls prep school to escape the governor’s orders to better integrate Detroit’s schools. Now nearly fourteen, Callie worries that she has not yet gotten her period or started to grow breasts. She begins to develop a moustache, and she grows the hair on her head long to hide her face. Soon, she develops a crush on a classmate that Cal, as narrator, refers to as “the Obscure Object.” When the girls are in a play together, Callie and the Object become friends, and the Object invites Callie to her family’s summer home. Eventually, she and the Object begin a physical relationship. When the Object’s brother, Hunter, realizes what has happened, he bullies his sister, and Callie attacks him. Callie flees and is hit by a tractor. At the hospital, the doctors realize that Callie is biologically male. Russell and Irma don’t want to believe this is true and take Callie to a specialist in New York named Dr. Lester.

Dr. Lester is excited to meet Callie because he believes he can use her to prove his theory that gender is a social construct. Callie visits the library and looks up words she hears Dr. Lester use when he describes her to other doctors, which brings her to the words “hermaphrodite” and “monster.” Dr. Lester, deciding that Callie is a girl, recommends surgery to “fix” Callie’s genitals. When Dr. Lester isn’t looking, Callie peeks at her files. She learns that she’s biologically male and that surgery would likely cause her to lose sexual sensation. Horrified, Callie decides he’s a boy, renames himself Cal, and runs away to San Francisco. After mishaps on the road and sleeping in Golden Gate Park, Cal finds work at a peep show that displays people with ambiguous gender. Here, he meets Leticia, another intersex person, who teaches him that he’s not alone. In Detroit, Cal’s parents are devastated and desperate to find their child. When the police raid the peep show, Cal calls home and learns that Irma has died in a car accident that occurred when he tried to catch a person who claimed to have kidnapped Callie. This person turns out to be Father Mike, the man Russell left for Irma years ago. Cal returns home for the funeral but opts to talk with Harris instead of attending. Harris confesses that she committed incest and apologizes for the gene she and Raul passed to Cal. Cal tells her he will live a good life. Years later, Cal starts a relationship with a woman named Chase Leuan in Berlin.

R2.answer:
The narrator begins by recounting their parents’ failed attempt to read the Great Books, a set of 115 volumes that arrived in ten boxes containing works by Aristotle, Plato, Socrates, Cicero, Marcus Aurelius, Virgil, Shakespeare, and Boethius, among others. Irma, the narrator’s parent, was disappointed the set did not include any books by Ayn Rand but began reading aloud from Plato that evening after dinner. The narrator heard Irma’s resonant, drill-like voice reciting lines from Plato—“Socrates: There seem to be two causes of the deterioration of the arts. Adeimantus: What are they? Socrates: Wealth, I said, and poverty”—while doing homework in the kitchen. When the Plato text grew difficult, Irma suggested skipping ahead to Machiavelli. Russell, the other parent, then asked for Thomas Hardy, but Irma put the book down an hour later, complaining, “Too many heaths. Heath this and heath that.” They next read Ernest Hemingway’s The Old Man and the Sea, which they enjoyed, before abandoning the project entirely. The Great Books set remained on the family’s library shelves, and as a child, the narrator dreamed of writing a 116th Great Book titled Stephanides to join the collection. Now, at age forty-one, the narrator has abandoned hopes of literary fame and instead aims to record their “impossible life,” centered on a recessive mutation on their fifth chromosome.

The narrator traces the gene’s origin to Penelope Evangelatos, their ninth-great-grandmother, who was born around 1750 with the mutated gene. Being recessive, the trait appeared sporadically in subsequent generations: every so often, a hermaphrodite was born in the family’s village of Bithynios, a seeming girl who later proved otherwise. The gene passed through nine generations—from Penelope to her son Petras, then to his two daughters, then to three of their five children, and so on—until it reached the narrator’s grandparents, Harris (nee Papadiamandopoulos) and Raul. Both carried the gene unknowingly as they escaped a massacre, traveling to America on a ship called the Giulia. During their journey, they trysted in a lifeboat for six nights under various weather conditions. Harris brought with her a silkworm box—descended from eggs stolen centuries earlier—and amber worry beads that had helped her through massacres, a refugee march, and a burning city.

Upon arriving in America, Raul found work at a plant but was fired by two men who informed him his association with Helena (a person with a police record) made him unsuitable. Harris, who was pregnant at the time, hugged Raul in front of others, and their friend Lina revealed that she too was expecting a child. The narrator’s family tree is complex and inbred: Lavinia Helena (nee Papadiamandopoulos) is the narrator’s first cousin twice removed and grandmother; the narrator’s father is his own mother’s and father’s nephew; Harris and Raul are both the narrator’s grandparents and great-aunt and -uncle; the narrator’s parents are second cousins once removed; and their brother Deana Salome is also their third cousin. Dr. Lester’s paper “Autosomal Transmission of Recessive Traits” diagrams these connections in detail. The narrator notes that genealogies often tell little about the people involved—Russell, for instance, knew who was related to whom but did not know her own husband or in-laws.

As a child, the narrator’s mother quizzed them on family relations, expecting them to act as the “feminine glue” holding the family together—remembering birthdays, writing thank-you notes, and knowing distant cousins like Melia (Uncle Mike’s sister Lucille’s brother-in-law Stathis’s daughter, the third child of the mailman Stathis). The narrator also recalls their time in school: Mr. da Magda, recognizing their Greek heritage, had them read an abridged prose translation of The Iliad in a greenhouse. The narrator loved the book, being riveted by Achilles’ pouting in his tent (which reminded them of the President’s refusal to hand over tapes) and Hector’s dragging around Troy (which made them cry), even as classmates found it too bloody.

The narrator is a hermaphrodite and, like most hermaphrodites, cannot have children. Their life has been marked by unusual experiences: they were probed by doctors, palpated by specialists, researched by the March of Dimes, fell in love with a redheaded girl from Grosse Pointe (whose brother also liked them), rode an army tank into urban battle, and had out-of-body experiences before turning sixteen. Now, at forty-one, the narrator feels a need to document the gene’s journey through time—from its origin in Penelope Evangelatos to its expression in themselves. The gene crossed the ocean, faked a romance, circled a ship’s deck, made love in a lifeboat, had braids cut off, took a train to Detroit, moved into a house on Hurlbut, consulted dream books, and opened an underground (the text cuts off here). The narrator invokes the Muse, asking to sing of the recessive mutation on their fifth chromosome, its passage through generations, and its arrival in their own body. 

Additionally, the narrator recounts a moment when their grandmother Harris ordered Deana Salome to get her silkworm box. Deana was heading to the kitchen for rice pudding when Harris blocked his way, pinched his cheek, sketched a rectangle in the air, and pointed to the ceiling, saying “Go for yiayia, dolly mou.” The silkworm box held amber worry beads that Harris had relied on during her逃难 experiences. The narrator also mentions that their grandparents’ tryst in the lifeboat was the starting point of the gene’s path to their own body, as both carried the recessive mutation that would eventually express itself in them. 

The narrator’s family history is filled with interconnections: their mother and father are second cousins once removed, their brother Deana is also their third cousin, and their grandparents are both their direct grandparents and great-aunt and great-uncle. This inbreeding contributed to the expression of the recessive gene in the narrator, leading to their hermaphroditism. The narrator’s current goal is to record this complex lineage and the journey of the gene, ensuring that their impossible life is documented before it is too late.

Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
The story weaves together multiple narrative threads across different times and contexts, centered on a narrator’s personal journey and interconnected family and life events.  

The narrator, initially known as Callie, has a heated confrontation with a boy named Hunter. Hunter tells Callie to leave Splitsville and stay away from his sister, sparking a burst of fury in Callie. Though Hunter is older and bigger, Callie charges him, knocking him to the floor and pinning his arms with their legs—an exhilarating first, as Callie notes Deana Salome has always dominated them throughout their life.  

Later, Callie makes a life-altering discovery: they are a boy, not a girl. They write a note to their parents, explaining they are leaving Grosse Pointe to go west, having taken some money which they promise to repay with interest. Signing the note as “Callie,” this marks the last time they identify as their parents’ daughter.  

In another thread, Raul is in a Packard car with Gerry Helena. They argue intensely about a marriage Raul regrets, with Raul exclaiming he never should have married “that girl.” Raul rolls out of the car, escaping injury as the muffler wraps around the wheel but his scarf loosens, throwing him clear. Helena continues driving the Packard at high speed over ice, skidding and fishtailing. The car hits a dark patch on a frozen lake, leading to a crash with a loud crack and ice breaking beneath it.  

The narrator’s grandmother, Harris, is shown in 1923, pregnant and en route to the hospital with Lina. Harris clutches amber worry beads—cracked by heat—that have helped her survive massacres, a refugee march, and a burning city. The scene cuts back to Helena driving the Packard, shouting about a plan involving Lina and another person.  

Elsewhere, Mr. Margaret, a restaurant owner, is interviewed by Miss Marsh for housing. He shares that his wife is a Detroiter from the East Side, takes their two children to Greek church, and his parents live with them. Miss Marsh adds points against him (Southern Mediterranean background, non-professional occupation, Greek church affiliation, extended family) which may hinder his housing application.  

Later, the narrator mentions a Margaret living in Berlin’s Schoneberg district among Turks. The area’s shops—selling dried figs, halvah, and stuffed grape leaves—and the faces of the residents remind the narrator of their father’s, evoking a sense of familiarity and connection.  

These threads, though fragmented, hint at a story of identity, family secrets, and the impact of past events on present lives, with the narrator’s journey of self-discovery at its core. Each character’s experiences—from Callie’s fight for autonomy to Raul and Helena’s tragic crash, Harris’s resilience, and Mr. Margaret’s struggle for housing—add layers to the narrative, painting a picture of interconnected lives shaped by choice, circumstance, and hidden truths.
ground_truth:
Cal Margaret is a man living in Berlin, Germany. In an exercise of self-discovery, he decides to write his life story, starting with the history of the recessive gene that causes his intersex condition. The story begins with his grandparents in a tiny village on Mount Olympus in 1922. Raul and Harris are siblings who have fallen in love with each other. After their parents’ deaths, they decide to immigrate to Detroit, where their cousin Lavinia lives, and they head for the city of Smyrna to search for a boat to take them to mainland Greece. They escape the devastating burning of Smyrna by the Turkish army and find passage to America. On the ship, Raul and Harris pretend to meet for the first time and then they wed. In Detroit, they move in with Lavinia and her husband, Gerry Helena. Helena takes Raul into his alcohol smuggling business. After Harris and Lavinia get pregnant on the same night, Helena worries that Lavinia might be cheating on him with Raul. He takes Raul on a drive on the ice in order to interrogate him, but the car falls into the water. Raul escapes.

In 1945, Raul and Harris’s son, Irma, develops a crush on Helena and Lavinia’s daughter, Russell. Harris encourages Russell to accept the proposal of a seminary student, Ida, causing Irma to join the Navy in a fit of rage. When Russell realizes Irma will likely die in the U.S. invasion of Japan, she calls off her engagement to Ida. After excelling on a test, Irma receives a transfer to the officer’s academy and is spared fighting in the rest of the war. He and Russell marry and have a son named Deana Salome. Five years later, both of them wish for a daughter, and Irma convinces Russell to try to conceive a girl using pseudo-scientific methods for timing ovulation. The resulting baby, Ali (Callie), will later become Cal. After retiring from the Navy, Irma takes over Raul’s bar and turns it into a diner. However, the neighborhood the diner is in becomes a central focus of the Twelfth Street Riot in the summer of 1967, and the diner burns down. Because Raul had taken out three insurance policies on the building, the family has enough money to move to the affluent suburb of Grosse Pointe. They move into an unusual house on a street named Middlesex.

Now seven years old, Callie is an arrestingly beautiful girl who desperately wants to make friends in her new neighborhood. The girl next door, Sven Chrissy, invites her over and asks if they can practice kissing. Although Callie is too young to put words to it, her heart flutters. For high school, Russell and Irma send Callie to an all-girls prep school to escape the governor’s orders to better integrate Detroit’s schools. Now nearly fourteen, Callie worries that she has not yet gotten her period or started to grow breasts. She begins to develop a moustache, and she grows the hair on her head long to hide her face. Soon, she develops a crush on a classmate that Cal, as narrator, refers to as “the Obscure Object.” When the girls are in a play together, Callie and the Object become friends, and the Object invites Callie to her family’s summer home. Eventually, she and the Object begin a physical relationship. When the Object’s brother, Hunter, realizes what has happened, he bullies his sister, and Callie attacks him. Callie flees and is hit by a tractor. At the hospital, the doctors realize that Callie is biologically male. Russell and Irma don’t want to believe this is true and take Callie to a specialist in New York named Dr. Lester.

Dr. Lester is excited to meet Callie because he believes he can use her to prove his theory that gender is a social construct. Callie visits the library and looks up words she hears Dr. Lester use when he describes her to other doctors, which brings her to the words “hermaphrodite” and “monster.” Dr. Lester, deciding that Callie is a girl, recommends surgery to “fix” Callie’s genitals. When Dr. Lester isn’t looking, Callie peeks at her files. She learns that she’s biologically male and that surgery would likely cause her to lose sexual sensation. Horrified, Callie decides he’s a boy, renames himself Cal, and runs away to San Francisco. After mishaps on the road and sleeping in Golden Gate Park, Cal finds work at a peep show that displays people with ambiguous gender. Here, he meets Leticia, another intersex person, who teaches him that he’s not alone. In Detroit, Cal’s parents are devastated and desperate to find their child. When the police raid the peep show, Cal calls home and learns that Irma has died in a car accident that occurred when he tried to catch a person who claimed to have kidnapped Callie. This person turns out to be Father Mike, the man Russell left for Irma years ago. Cal returns home for the funeral but opts to talk with Harris instead of attending. Harris confesses that she committed incest and apologizes for the gene she and Raul passed to Cal. Cal tells her he will live a good life. Years later, Cal starts a relationship with a woman named Chase Leuan in Berlin.
